{"description":"Design and build Abacus: a standalone SQLite-backed graph tool that models an application's API surface, entities, pages, and user actions. Serves as the persistent, cumulative codebase understanding layer that bridges Gherkin specs to implementation. Standalone project (new Linear project: Abacus) with optional beads integration via hooks.

Linear issue: https://linear.app/pcom-mike/issue/ABA-1/brainstorm-abacus-living-application-graph-gherkin-driven-bdd

## Core Architecture
- SQLite database (.abacus/abacus.db)
- CLI tool (abacus or ab)
- MCP server for Claude Code integration
- Auto-scan scripts for structural layer
- Agent enrichment protocol for semantic layer

## Graph Node Types (Launch)
1. Routes — auto-scanned from oRPC contracts (method, path, input/output schemas)
2. Entities — auto-scanned from Prisma schema (models, fields, relations)
3. Pages — auto-scanned from React Router config (URL, components)
4. Actions — agent-enriched semantic bridge (gherkin_patterns, api_route, entities, page, permissions, ui_hints)
5. Permissions — hybrid (auto-scan ACL config + agent enrichment)

## The BDD Flow
Gherkin step → abacus actions --match → resolves to Action node → Action tells agent the API route, entities, page, and permissions → agent generates E2E test + implementation

## Population Strategy
- Hybrid: auto-scan + agent enrichment
- abacus scan rebuilds structural layer from code
- Agents enrich Actions as they work through features
- Actions are the cumulative knowledge — they persist and grow

## Beads Integration (optional)
- Hooks connect beads and abacus when both are present
- When a bead is worked on, agents can annotate which Actions/Routes were touched
- Graph data can feed into bead descriptions","priority":1,"title":"Brainstorm: Abacus — Living Application Graph + Gherkin-driven BDD"}

{"id":"aba-zca","title":"Abacus: Living Application Graph + Gherkin-driven BDD","description":"Build Abacus: a standalone SQLite-backed graph tool that models an application's API surface, entities, pages, and user actions. Serves as the persistent, cumulative codebase understanding layer that bridges Gherkin specs to implementation.

Linear project: Abacus (pcom-mike workspace)
Linear issue: https://linear.app/pcom-mike/issue/ABA-1/brainstorm-abacus-living-application-graph-gherkin-driven-bdd

## Architecture

- **Database**: SQLite (.abacus/abacus.db) per target project, WAL mode, JSONB properties, FTS5
- **Monorepo**: pnpm workspaces — packages/core, packages/cli, packages/mcp-server
- **Graph Model**: Adjacency list (nodes + edges tables) with 5 node types:
  - Routes (auto-scanned from oRPC contracts)
  - Entities (auto-scanned from Prisma schema)
  - Pages (auto-scanned from React Router config)
  - Actions (agent-enriched semantic bridge)
  - Permissions (hybrid: auto-scan + enrichment)
- **Scanner Architecture**: Pluggable Scanner interface with implementations for oRPC, Prisma, React Router
- **Match Algorithm**: Tiered — Cucumber expressions → FTS5 fuzzy → LLM-assisted Action creation
- **MCP Server**: @modelcontextprotocol/server via stdio transport
- **CLI**: Commander.js + @commander-js/extra-typings

## Technology Stack
- TypeScript (strict, ES2022, bundler moduleResolution)
- pnpm 10+, Node 24+
- better-sqlite3 for database
- ts-morph for AST parsing (oRPC, Router)
- @prisma/internals getDMMF for Prisma schema
- @cucumber/gherkin + @cucumber/expression for Gherkin parsing & matching
- @modelcontextprotocol/server for MCP
- Zod v4 for validation

## Key Decisions
1. Standalone tool, not embedded in beads — optional hooks integration
2. .abacus/ directory lives in the TARGET project (like .beads/)
3. Graph schema is API-first with Action semantic layer
4. Full BDD cycle: Gherkin → E2E test → implementation
5. Match algorithm is a GROWTH mechanism — LLM fallback creates new Actions
6. Scanners are pluggable from day 1

## Phases
1. Foundation (scaffolding, schema, scanner arch)
2. Scanners (oRPC, Prisma, Router — parallelizable)
3. Core Features (query layer, actions, match algorithm)
4. Interfaces (CLI commands, MCP server)
5. Integration (E2E validation against hourly codebase)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mike@seedless.club","created_at":"2026-03-06T20:49:55Z","created_by":"mjn","updated_at":"2026-03-06T20:49:55Z"}

{"description":"Build Abacus: a standalone SQLite-backed graph tool that models an application's API surface, entities, pages, and user actions. Serves as the persistent, cumulative codebase understanding layer that bridges Gherkin specs to implementation.

Linear project: Abacus (pcom-mike workspace)
Linear issue: https://linear.app/pcom-mike/issue/ABA-1/brainstorm-abacus-living-application-graph-gherkin-driven-bdd

## Architecture

- **Language**: Go (single binary, excellent compiler feedback, matches beads tooling)
- **Database**: SQLite (.abacus/abacus.db) per target project via modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go, no CGo)
- **CLI**: cobra (same as beads)
- **MCP Server**: github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk (official Go SDK)
- **Graph Model**: Adjacency list (nodes + edges tables) with JSONB properties, FTS5 search
- **Node Types**: Route, Entity, Page, Action, Permission

## BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) Scanner Architecture

Abacus does NOT parse source code itself. Instead:
- Abacus defines a **Scanner Protocol** — a JSON schema for scan results
- Scanners are **standalone executables** that output JSON conforming to the protocol
- Abacus spawns scanners as subprocesses, reads their JSON output, ingests into SQLite
- Config (.abacus/config.yaml) maps scanner IDs to executable commands

This means:
- Abacus core is 100% Go — no TypeScript, no AST parsing, no language-specific deps
- Scanners can be written in ANY language (Node.js for TS, Python for Django, etc.)
- We ship 4 reference scanners as npm packages (oRPC, Prisma, React Router, Express)
- Community can write scanners for any stack

## Project Structure
\`\`\`
abacus/
├── go.mod / go.sum
├── cmd/abacus/main.go           # CLI + MCP server entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── db/                      # SQLite connection, schema, migrations
│   ├── graph/                   # GraphRepository, ActionService
│   ├── match/                   # MatchService, CucumberMatcher, FuzzyMatcher, SuggestionBuilder
│   ├── scanner/                 # Scanner protocol types, subprocess runner
│   └── cli/                     # Cobra command definitions
├── mcp/                         # MCP server tool/resource registration
├── scanners/                    # Reference scanner implementations (Node.js)
│   ├── orpc/                    # @abacus/scanner-orpc
│   ├── prisma/                  # @abacus/scanner-prisma
│   ├── react-router/            # @abacus/scanner-react-router
│   └── express/                 # @abacus/scanner-express
└── testdata/                    # Test fixtures
\`\`\`

## Technology Stack
- Go 1.23+ (single binary distribution)
- modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go SQLite, no CGo)
- github.com/spf13/cobra (CLI framework)
- github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk (MCP server)
- github.com/cucumber/cucumber-expressions-go (pattern matching)
- github.com/cucumber/gherkin/go (Gherkin parsing)
- Node.js scanner packages: ts-morph, @prisma/internals, @cucumber/gherkin

## Key Decisions
1. Go for core — single binary, great compiler messages, matches beads ecosystem
2. BYOASTP — scanners are standalone executables, not embedded parsers
3. .abacus/ directory lives in the TARGET project (like .beads/)
4. Scanner Protocol is the interface — JSON in/out, language-agnostic
5. Match algorithm is a GROWTH mechanism — LLM fallback creates new Actions
6. 4 reference scanners ship as npm packages for the hourly stack
7. Official Go MCP SDK for Claude Code integration

## Phases
1. Foundation (scaffolding, schema, scanner protocol)
2. Scanners (oRPC, Prisma, Router, Express — Node.js, parallelizable)
3. Core Features (query layer, actions, match algorithm)
4. Interfaces (CLI commands, MCP server)
5. Integration (E2E validation against hourly codebase)"}

{"description":"## What

Set up the Abacus Go module with directory structure, cobra CLI skeleton, build tooling, and configuration schema. This is the foundation all other beads build on.

## Context

Abacus is a Go project producing a single binary. It uses cobra for CLI (same as beads), modernc.org/sqlite for the database, and the official Go MCP SDK. The .abacus/ directory lives in the TARGET project, not in the Abacus repo.

## Implementation

### Directory Structure
\`\`\`
abacus/
├── go.mod                          # module github.com/mjn/abacus
├── go.sum
├── Makefile                        # build, test, install targets
├── cmd/abacus/main.go              # entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── db/                         # SQLite layer
│   ├── graph/                      # Graph operations
│   ├── match/                      # Match algorithm
│   ├── scanner/                    # Scanner protocol + runner
│   └── cli/                        # Cobra commands
├── mcp/                            # MCP server
├── scanners/                       # Reference Node.js scanners
│   ├── orpc/
│   ├── prisma/
│   ├── react-router/
│   └── express/
└── testdata/                       # Test fixtures
\`\`\`

### go.mod
\`\`\`go
module github.com/mjn/abacus

go 1.23

require (
    github.com/spf13/cobra v1.9.1
    modernc.org/sqlite v1.37.1
    github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk v1.4.0
    github.com/cucumber/cucumber-expressions-go v0.0.0
    github.com/cucumber/gherkin/go/v38 v38.0.0
)
\`\`\`

### Makefile
- \`make build\` — \`go build -o bin/abacus ./cmd/abacus\`
- \`make install\` — \`go install ./cmd/abacus\`
- \`make test\` — \`go test ./...\`
- \`make lint\` — \`golangci-lint run\`

### Entry Point (cmd/abacus/main.go)
\`\`\`go
package main

import (
    "os"
    "github.com/mjn/abacus/internal/cli"
)

func main() {
    if err := cli.Execute(); err != nil {
        os.Exit(1)
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Configuration Schema (.abacus/config.yaml)
\`\`\`yaml
version: 1
project:
  name: "hourly"
  root: "."
scanners:
  orpc:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-orpc"
    options:
      contractPaths: ["shared/api-client/src/contracts/**/*.ts"]
      handlerPaths: ["backend/src/orpc/**/*.handlers.ts"]
  prisma:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-prisma"
    options:
      schemaPath: "backend/prisma/schema.prisma"
  react-router:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-react-router"
    options:
      configPath: "frontend/src/App.tsx"
      pagesDir: "frontend/src/pages"
  express:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-express"
    options:
      routePaths: ["backend/src/routes/**/*.ts"]
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] \`go build ./...\` compiles without errors
- [ ] \`go vet ./...\` passes
- [ ] \`make build\` produces bin/abacus binary
- [ ] \`make install\` installs abacus to GOPATH/bin
- [ ] \`abacus --version\` prints version
- [ ] \`abacus --help\` prints help
- [ ] Config YAML parsing works for valid config
- [ ] Config parsing rejects invalid YAML with clear errors

## Validation

- [ ] Single binary builds in < 5 seconds
- [ ] Binary size is reasonable (< 30MB)
- [ ] All directories exist and Go packages compile
- [ ] No CGo dependencies (pure Go)

## Files

- go.mod, go.sum
- Makefile
- cmd/abacus/main.go
- internal/cli/root.go
- internal/config/config.go (config parsing)
- .gitignore

## Dependencies

None — this is the first bead."}

{"id":"aba-zca.1","title":"Phase 1: Project Scaffolding & Configuration","description":"## What

Set up the Abacus monorepo with pnpm workspaces, TypeScript configuration, and project-level tooling. This is the foundation all other beads build on.

## Context

Abacus is a standalone tool separate from the hourly codebase. It will be installed globally or as a devDependency in target projects. The .abacus/ directory (containing abacus.db + config) lives in the TARGET project, not in the Abacus repo itself.

Reference patterns from the hourly monorepo: pnpm 10+, Node 24+, TypeScript strict mode, ES2022 target, bundler moduleResolution.

## Implementation

### Directory Structure
\`\`\`
abacus/
├── package.json                    # Root workspace config
├── pnpm-workspace.yaml             # Workspace definition
├── tsconfig.base.json              # Shared TS config
├── packages/
│   ├── core/
│   │   ├── package.json            # name: @abacus/core
│   │   ├── tsconfig.json           # extends base
│   │   └── src/
│   ├── cli/
│   │   ├── package.json            # name: @abacus/cli, bin: { abacus, ab }
│   │   ├── tsconfig.json
│   │   └── src/
│   └── mcp-server/
│       ├── package.json            # name: @abacus/mcp-server
│       ├── tsconfig.json
│       └── src/
└── .gitignore
\`\`\`

### Root package.json
- type: "module"
- engines: { node: ">=24.0.0" }
- packageManager: pnpm
- Scripts: build, test, lint, typecheck
- devDependencies: typescript (latest), @types/node, vitest

### tsconfig.base.json
- target: ES2022, module: es2022, moduleResolution: bundler
- strict: true, noUnusedLocals: true, noUnusedParameters: true
- skipLibCheck: true, declaration: true, sourceMap: true
- paths for workspace package references

### pnpm-workspace.yaml
\`\`\`yaml
packages:
  - packages/*
\`\`\`

### Package dependencies (install in correct packages)
- @abacus/core: better-sqlite3, @types/better-sqlite3, ts-morph, @prisma/internals, @cucumber/gherkin, @cucumber/messages, zod
- @abacus/cli: commander, @commander-js/extra-typings, @abacus/core (workspace:*)
- @abacus/mcp-server: @modelcontextprotocol/server, @abacus/core (workspace:*)

### Configuration Schema (.abacus/config.yaml)
Define what the init command creates — scanner paths, project metadata:
\`\`\`yaml
version: 1
project:
  name: "hourly"
  root: "."
scanners:
  orpc:
    enabled: true
    contractPaths: ["shared/api-client/src/contracts/**/*.ts"]
    handlerPaths: ["backend/src/orpc/**/*.handlers.ts"]
  prisma:
    enabled: true
    schemaPath: "backend/prisma/schema.prisma"
  router:
    enabled: true
    configPath: "frontend/src/App.tsx"
    pagesDir: "frontend/src/pages"
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] pnpm install succeeds with no errors
- [ ] pnpm build compiles all 3 packages
- [ ] pnpm typecheck passes strict mode
- [ ] @abacus/core can be imported from @abacus/cli
- [ ] @abacus/core can be imported from @abacus/mcp-server
- [ ] bin entry (abacus, ab) resolves to CLI entry point
- [ ] Config schema validates correct YAML and rejects malformed YAML

## Validation

- [ ] All packages build without errors
- [ ] Workspace cross-references resolve correctly
- [ ] TypeScript strict mode passes
- [ ] CLI binary is executable after build

## Files

- package.json (root)
- pnpm-workspace.yaml
- tsconfig.base.json
- .gitignore
- packages/core/package.json
- packages/core/tsconfig.json
- packages/core/src/index.ts
- packages/cli/package.json
- packages/cli/tsconfig.json
- packages/cli/src/index.ts
- packages/mcp-server/package.json
- packages/mcp-server/tsconfig.json
- packages/mcp-server/src/index.ts

## Dependencies

None — this is the first bead.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mike@seedless.club","created_at":"2026-03-06T20:50:19Z","created_by":"mjn","updated_at":"2026-03-06T20:50:19Z"}

{"description":"## What

Design and implement the SQLite database schema and Go type system for the Abacus application graph. Includes all table definitions, indexes, FTS5, and the DB connection module.

## Context

Same SQL schema as before — the database design is language-independent. The Go layer uses modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go, standard database/sql interface). WAL mode for concurrent reads, NORMAL synchronous for CLI speed.

## Implementation

### Go Types (internal/db/types.go)
\`\`\`go
package db

type NodeKind string
const (
    NodeRoute      NodeKind = "route"
    NodeEntity     NodeKind = "entity"
    NodePage       NodeKind = "page"
    NodeAction     NodeKind = "action"
    NodePermission NodeKind = "permission"
)

type NodeSource string
const (
    SourceScan   NodeSource = "scan"
    SourceAgent  NodeSource = "agent"
    SourceManual NodeSource = "manual"
)

type EdgeKind string
const (
    EdgeUsesRoute          EdgeKind = "uses_route"
    EdgeTouchesEntity      EdgeKind = "touches_entity"
    EdgeOnPage             EdgeKind = "on_page"
    EdgeRequiresPermission EdgeKind = "requires_permission"
    EdgeRelatesTo          EdgeKind = "relates_to"
    EdgeFieldRelation      EdgeKind = "field_relation"
)

type GraphNode struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    Kind       NodeKind       \`json:"kind"\`
    Name       string         \`json:"name"\`
    Label      string         \`json:"label"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    Source     NodeSource     \`json:"source"\`
    SourceFile *string        \`json:"source_file"\`
    CreatedAt  int64          \`json:"created_at"\`
    UpdatedAt  int64          \`json:"updated_at"\`
    ScanHash   *string        \`json:"scan_hash"\`
}

type GraphEdge struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    SrcID      string         \`json:"src_id"\`
    DstID      string         \`json:"dst_id"\`
    Kind       EdgeKind       \`json:"kind"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    CreatedAt  int64          \`json:"created_at"\`
}
\`\`\`

### DB Connection (internal/db/db.go)
- OpenDB(path string) (*sql.DB, error) — open with WAL mode, foreign keys, cache
- InitSchema(db *sql.DB) error — create tables, indexes, FTS5, triggers
- CloseDB(db *sql.DB) error — graceful close
- Properties stored as JSON TEXT (modernc.org/sqlite supports json_extract)

### SQL Schema
Same as original plan — nodes, edges, nodes_fts, triggers, schema_version. See epic description for full DDL.

## Testing

- [ ] OpenDB creates database with correct pragmas
- [ ] InitSchema creates all tables and indexes
- [ ] Node CRUD operations work for all 5 kinds
- [ ] Edge CRUD with UNIQUE constraint enforcement
- [ ] FTS5 search with BM25 ranking
- [ ] JSON properties round-trip correctly
- [ ] Cascading edge deletion on node delete
- [ ] Schema versioning works
- [ ] Transaction rollback on error

## Validation

- [ ] All 5 node types stored and retrieved correctly
- [ ] Edges maintain referential integrity
- [ ] FTS5 returns relevant results
- [ ] Pure Go — no CGo compilation required

## Files

- internal/db/types.go
- internal/db/db.go
- internal/db/schema.go (DDL strings)
- internal/db/db_test.go

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.1 (Go module must exist)"}

{"description":"## What

Design and implement the Scanner Protocol — the BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) interface that defines how external scanner executables communicate with Abacus. This is the most architecturally important bead: it defines the contract that makes scanners language-agnostic.

## Context

Abacus does NOT parse source code. Instead, scanners are standalone executables that:
1. Receive configuration via stdin (JSON)
2. Parse source files in whatever language they understand
3. Output a ScanResult JSON document to stdout
4. Exit 0 on success, non-zero on failure (errors to stderr)

The Scanner Protocol is a versioned JSON schema. Abacus ships v1; future versions maintain backward compatibility.

## Implementation

### Scanner Protocol v1 — The Interface

**Input (stdin → scanner):**
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": 1,
  "projectRoot": "/absolute/path/to/project",
  "options": {
    "contractPaths": ["shared/api-client/src/contracts/**/*.ts"]
  }
}
\`\`\`

**Output (scanner → stdout):**
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": 1,
  "scanner": {
    "id": "orpc",
    "name": "oRPC Route Scanner",
    "version": "0.1.0"
  },
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "route:POST-/api/auth/register",
      "kind": "route",
      "name": "POST /api/auth/register",
      "label": "Register a new user",
      "properties": {
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/api/auth/register",
        "summary": "Register a new user",
        "tags": ["Auth"],
        "inputSchema": "RegisterInputSchema",
        "outputSchema": "AuthResponseSchema",
        "successStatus": 201,
        "contractFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts",
        "handlerFile": "backend/src/orpc/auth.handlers.ts"
      },
      "source": "scan",
      "sourceFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts"
    }
  ],
  "edges": [
    {
      "id": "edge:entity:User-field_relation-entity:Company",
      "srcId": "entity:User",
      "dstId": "entity:Company",
      "kind": "field_relation",
      "properties": {
        "fieldName": "company",
        "relationType": "many-to-one"
      }
    }
  ],
  "warnings": [
    {
      "file": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/broken.ts",
      "line": 42,
      "message": "Failed to parse: unexpected token",
      "severity": "warn"
    }
  ],
  "stats": {
    "filesScanned": 45,
    "nodesFound": 47,
    "edgesFound": 12,
    "errors": 1,
    "durationMs": 1234
  }
}
\`\`\`

### Go Types for Protocol (internal/scanner/protocol.go)

\`\`\`go
package scanner

// ScanInput is sent to scanner executables via stdin
type ScanInput struct {
    Version     int            \`json:"version"\`
    ProjectRoot string         \`json:"projectRoot"\`
    Options     map[string]any \`json:"options"\`
}

// ScanOutput is received from scanner executables via stdout
type ScanOutput struct {
    Version  int            \`json:"version"\`
    Scanner  ScannerInfo    \`json:"scanner"\`
    Nodes    []ScanNode     \`json:"nodes"\`
    Edges    []ScanEdge     \`json:"edges"\`
    Warnings []ScanWarning  \`json:"warnings"\`
    Stats    ScanStats      \`json:"stats"\`
}

type ScannerInfo struct {
    ID      string \`json:"id"\`
    Name    string \`json:"name"\`
    Version string \`json:"version"\`
}

type ScanNode struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    Kind       string         \`json:"kind"\`   // must be valid NodeKind
    Name       string         \`json:"name"\`
    Label      string         \`json:"label"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    Source     string         \`json:"source"\` // always "scan" for scanners
    SourceFile string         \`json:"sourceFile,omitempty"\`
}

type ScanEdge struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    SrcID      string         \`json:"srcId"\`
    DstID      string         \`json:"dstId"\`
    Kind       string         \`json:"kind"\`   // must be valid EdgeKind
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties,omitempty"\`
}

type ScanWarning struct {
    File     string \`json:"file"\`
    Line     *int   \`json:"line,omitempty"\`
    Message  string \`json:"message"\`
    Severity string \`json:"severity"\` // "info", "warn", "error"
}

type ScanStats struct {
    FilesScanned int   \`json:"filesScanned"\`
    NodesFound   int   \`json:"nodesFound"\`
    EdgesFound   int   \`json:"edgesFound"\`
    Errors       int   \`json:"errors"\`
    DurationMs   int64 \`json:"durationMs"\`
}
\`\`\`

### Scanner Runner (internal/scanner/runner.go)

\`\`\`go
package scanner

// Runner spawns scanner executables and collects results
type Runner struct {
    timeout time.Duration  // default: 60s
}

// RunScanner spawns a scanner executable, sends input via stdin, reads output from stdout
func (r *Runner) RunScanner(ctx context.Context, command string, input ScanInput) (*ScanOutput, error)

// RunAll runs all configured scanners (sequentially or parallel) and merges results
func (r *Runner) RunAll(ctx context.Context, configs []ScannerConfig) (*MergedScanOutput, error)

// ValidateOutput checks that scanner output conforms to protocol v1
func ValidateOutput(output *ScanOutput) []string  // returns validation errors
\`\`\`

**RunScanner implementation:**
1. Parse command string into executable + args
2. Create exec.CommandContext with timeout
3. Pipe ScanInput JSON to stdin
4. Capture stdout (ScanOutput JSON) and stderr (error messages)
5. Parse and validate the JSON output
6. Return ScanOutput or error with stderr contents

**Error handling:**
- Scanner exits non-zero → return error with stderr
- Scanner produces invalid JSON → return parse error with raw output
- Scanner times out → kill process, return timeout error
- Scanner outputs valid JSON with warnings → return output + log warnings

### Protocol Validation Rules
- version must be 1
- scanner.id must be non-empty
- All nodes must have id, kind, name, label
- kind must be one of: route, entity, page, action, permission
- All edges must reference node IDs that exist in the nodes array
- Edge kind must be valid EdgeKind
- No duplicate node IDs

### JSON Schema File
Generate a JSON Schema file (protocol/scanner-v1.schema.json) that scanner authors can validate against. This serves as documentation AND validation tool.

## Testing

- [ ] RunScanner spawns a test executable and reads its output
- [ ] RunScanner sends ScanInput JSON via stdin correctly
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner exit code 0 → success
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner exit code 1 → error with stderr
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner timeout → kills process
- [ ] RunScanner handles invalid JSON output → clear error message
- [ ] ValidateOutput accepts valid ScanOutput
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects output with missing required fields
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects output with invalid node kinds
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects edges referencing nonexistent nodes
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects duplicate node IDs
- [ ] RunAll runs multiple scanners and merges results
- [ ] RunAll continues when one scanner fails, reports error in warnings
- [ ] ScanInput JSON serialization is correct
- [ ] ScanOutput JSON deserialization handles all field types
- [ ] Test with a simple bash scanner: \`echo '{"version":1,...}'\`

## Validation

- [ ] Protocol is simple enough that a bash one-liner can be a valid scanner
- [ ] Protocol JSON schema is valid and documents all fields
- [ ] Runner handles real-world edge cases (large output, slow scanners)
- [ ] No Go-specific assumptions in the protocol (truly language-agnostic)
- [ ] Merged output from multiple scanners has no duplicate nodes

## Files

- internal/scanner/protocol.go (types)
- internal/scanner/runner.go (subprocess execution)
- internal/scanner/validate.go (output validation)
- internal/scanner/runner_test.go
- internal/scanner/validate_test.go
- protocol/scanner-v1.schema.json (JSON Schema for scanner authors)
- testdata/scanners/echo-scanner.sh (test fixture)

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.2 (needs db types for NodeKind, EdgeKind validation)"}

{"description":"## What

Implement the oRPC Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-orpc) that parses oRPC contract files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON.

## Context

This is a BYOASTP scanner — a standalone Node.js executable that:
1. Reads ScanInput JSON from stdin (projectRoot, options.contractPaths, options.handlerPaths)
2. Uses ts-morph to parse TypeScript AST and extract route definitions
3. Outputs ScanOutput JSON to stdout conforming to Scanner Protocol v1
4. Exits 0 on success

The target codebase has 45+ oRPC contracts like:
\`\`\`typescript
export const registerAuth = oc
  .route({ method: "POST", path: "/auth/register", summary: "Register a new user", tags: ["Auth"], successStatus: 201 })
  .input(RegisterInputSchema)
  .output(AuthResponseSchema);
\`\`\`

## Implementation

### Package Structure (scanners/orpc/)
\`\`\`
scanners/orpc/
├── package.json       # name: @abacus/scanner-orpc, bin: { abacus-scanner-orpc: "./dist/index.js" }
├── tsconfig.json
├── src/
│   ├── index.ts       # Entry point: read stdin, parse, write stdout
│   └── parser.ts      # ts-morph AST parsing logic
└── __tests__/
    └── parser.test.ts
\`\`\`

### Entry Point (src/index.ts)
\`\`\`typescript
#!/usr/bin/env node
import { readFileSync } from 'fs';

// Read ScanInput from stdin
const input = JSON.parse(readFileSync('/dev/stdin', 'utf8'));

// Parse contracts
const result = await parseOrpcContracts(input.projectRoot, input.options);

// Write ScanOutput to stdout
console.log(JSON.stringify(result));
\`\`\`

### Parser (src/parser.ts)
- Use ts-morph to find .route() call expressions
- Extract method, path, summary, tags, successStatus from object literal
- Follow chain to .input() and .output() to get schema names
- Generate node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{path}\`
- Compute scan hash per file (SHA-256 of file content)

### Output Example
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": 1,
  "scanner": { "id": "orpc", "name": "oRPC Route Scanner", "version": "0.1.0" },
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "route:POST-/api/auth/register",
      "kind": "route",
      "name": "POST /api/auth/register",
      "label": "Register a new user",
      "properties": {
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/api/auth/register",
        "summary": "Register a new user",
        "tags": ["Auth"],
        "inputSchema": "RegisterInputSchema",
        "outputSchema": "AuthResponseSchema",
        "successStatus": 201,
        "contractFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts"
      },
      "source": "scan",
      "sourceFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts"
    }
  ],
  "edges": [],
  "warnings": [],
  "stats": { "filesScanned": 45, "nodesFound": 47, "edgesFound": 0, "errors": 0, "durationMs": 1500 }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] Parses simple .route() call → correct node
- [ ] Handles all HTTP methods (POST, GET, PUT, DELETE, PATCH)
- [ ] Extracts tags, summary, successStatus
- [ ] Extracts input/output schema names
- [ ] Handles contracts without input or output
- [ ] Handles multiple contracts per file
- [ ] Generates deterministic IDs
- [ ] Skips files with syntax errors, reports warning
- [ ] Reads stdin, writes valid ScanOutput JSON to stdout
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly contract files via fixtures
- [ ] Conforms to Scanner Protocol v1 schema

## Validation

- [ ] All 45+ hourly contracts parsed successfully
- [ ] Output is valid Scanner Protocol v1 JSON
- [ ] \`echo '{"version":1,"projectRoot":"/path","options":{...}}' | npx @abacus/scanner-orpc\` works

## Files

- scanners/orpc/package.json
- scanners/orpc/tsconfig.json
- scanners/orpc/src/index.ts
- scanners/orpc/src/parser.ts
- scanners/orpc/__tests__/parser.test.ts
- scanners/orpc/__tests__/fixtures/ (contract fixtures)

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.3 (must know Scanner Protocol v1 schema)"}

{"id":"aba-zca.4","title":"Phase 2: oRPC Route Scanner","description":"## What

Implement the oRPC Route Scanner that parses oRPC contract files and extracts Route nodes for the application graph. This scanner reads TypeScript files containing oRPC route definitions and produces structured Route nodes with method, path, input/output schemas, tags, and handler file references.

## Context

The target codebase (hourly) has 45+ oRPC contracts in \`shared/api-client/src/contracts/*.ts\` with corresponding handlers in \`backend/src/orpc/*.handlers.ts\`.

oRPC contract pattern (from repo research):
\`\`\`typescript
export const registerAuth = oc
  .route({
    method: "POST",
    path: "/auth/register",
    summary: "Register a new user",
    tags: ["Auth"],
    successStatus: 201,
  })
  .input(RegisterInputSchema)    // Zod schema
  .output(AuthResponseSchema);
\`\`\`

The scanner must:
1. Find all contract files via configured glob patterns
2. Parse each file's AST using ts-morph
3. Extract route definitions by finding \`.route()\` call expressions
4. Extract method, path, summary, tags, successStatus from the route config object
5. Extract input/output schema names (not full schemas — just the identifier names)
6. Compute scan hash per file for change detection
7. Generate deterministic node IDs: \`route:{method}-{path}\`

### AST Parsing Strategy
Use ts-morph for this scanner because we need to navigate call expression chains (\`.route().input().output()\`). The pattern is:
1. Find all variable declarations with initializers
2. Follow the method chain to find \`.route()\` calls
3. Extract the object literal argument from \`.route()\`
4. Follow the chain to find \`.input()\` and \`.output()\` calls
5. Get the identifier name of the schema argument

### Edge Cases
- Contracts that use intermediate variables (not inline chaining)
- Contracts with multiple routes in one file
- Contracts without input or output schemas
- Files that import and re-export contracts
- Files with TypeScript syntax errors (skip with warning)

## Testing

- [ ] Scanner parses a simple contract with method, path, summary → produces correct Route node
- [ ] Scanner handles POST, GET, PUT, DELETE, PATCH methods
- [ ] Scanner extracts tags array correctly
- [ ] Scanner extracts input schema name (e.g., "RegisterInputSchema")
- [ ] Scanner extracts output schema name
- [ ] Scanner handles contracts without input schema (query-only)
- [ ] Scanner handles contracts without output schema
- [ ] Scanner handles multiple contracts per file
- [ ] Scanner generates deterministic IDs (same file → same node IDs)
- [ ] Scanner computes scan hash — same content produces same hash
- [ ] Scanner detects changed files via hash comparison
- [ ] Scanner skips files with syntax errors and reports warning
- [ ] Scanner resolves glob patterns correctly
- [ ] Integration test: parse actual hourly contract files (use fixtures)

## Validation

- [ ] All 45+ hourly oRPC contracts are successfully scanned
- [ ] Each Route node has: method, path, summary, tags, input/output schema names
- [ ] No duplicate node IDs
- [ ] Scan completes in under 5 seconds for the full contract set
- [ ] Scanner implements the Scanner interface correctly

## Files

- packages/core/src/scanner/orpc-scanner.ts
- packages/core/src/__tests__/scanner/orpc-scanner.test.ts
- packages/core/src/__tests__/fixtures/contracts/ (test fixture files)

## Dependencies

{"id":"aba-zca.5","title":"Phase 2: Prisma Entity Scanner","description":"## What

Implement the Prisma Entity Scanner that parses Prisma schema files and extracts Entity nodes with their fields, relations, enums, and table mappings.

## Context

The target codebase has 100+ Prisma models in \`backend/prisma/schema.prisma\`. The scanner uses \`@prisma/internals\` getDMMF() which gives us the Data Model Meta Format — a structured representation of the entire schema.

### Prisma DMMF approach
Instead of regex or AST parsing (Prisma files aren't TypeScript), use the official \`@prisma/internals\` package:
\`\`\`typescript
import { getDMMF } from '@prisma/internals';
const dmmf = await getDMMF({ datamodelPath: schemaPath });
// dmmf.datamodel.models — all models
// dmmf.datamodel.enums — all enums
\`\`\`

### What to extract per Entity
- Model name → node name and label
- Fields: name, type, isRequired, isId, isUnique, default value, documentation
- Relations: name, type (1:1, 1:N, M:N), target model, foreign key field
- Table mapping: @@map("table_name") if present
- Schema file path for source tracking

### Edges to create
- \`field_relation\` edges between Entity nodes that have Prisma relations
  - e.g., User has many Timecards → edge from entity:User to entity:Timecard with kind=field_relation

### Enum handling
- Create Entity nodes for enums with kind='entity' and a property flag \`isEnum: true\`
- Fields list contains the enum values

## Testing

- [ ] Scanner parses a simple model with scalar fields → correct Entity node
- [ ] Scanner extracts all field attributes (required, id, unique, default)
- [ ] Scanner identifies relations and creates edges between entities
- [ ] Scanner handles one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many relations
- [ ] Scanner extracts enums as Entity nodes with isEnum=true
- [ ] Scanner handles @@map table name overrides
- [ ] Scanner generates deterministic IDs: \`entity:{ModelName}\`
- [ ] Scanner computes scan hash from schema file content
- [ ] Scanner handles schema with syntax errors gracefully
- [ ] Integration test: parse actual hourly Prisma schema

## Validation

- [ ] All 100+ hourly Prisma models are successfully scanned
- [ ] Relations produce correct edges in the graph
- [ ] Enum values are captured
- [ ] Scan completes in under 3 seconds
- [ ] Scanner implements the Scanner interface correctly

## Files

- packages/core/src/scanner/prisma-scanner.ts
- packages/core/src/__tests__/scanner/prisma-scanner.test.ts
- packages/core/src/__tests__/fixtures/schema.prisma (test fixture)

## Dependencies

{"id":"aba-zca.6","title":"Phase 2: React Router Page Scanner","description":"## What

Implement the React Router Page Scanner that parses React Router configuration and extracts Page nodes with URL patterns, component mappings, protection status, and layout hierarchy.

## Context

The target codebase uses React Router DOM with JSX \`<Route>\` components in \`frontend/src/App.tsx\`. Routes include:
- Public routes: /, /login, /jobs, /contact
- Protected routes: require auth + onboarding check
- Nested routes under \`<MainApp />\` component

Pattern from repo research:
\`\`\`tsx
<Routes>
  <Route path="/" element={<LandingPage />} />
  <Route path="/jobs" element={<JobBoard />} />
  <Route path="/job/:id" element={<JobDetail />} />
  <Route path="/*" element={user ? <MainApp /> : <Navigate to="/login" />} />
</Routes>
\`\`\`

### AST Parsing Strategy
Use ts-morph for JSX parsing:
1. Find \`<Route>\` JSX elements
2. Extract \`path\` attribute (string literal)
3. Extract \`element\` attribute (JSX expression — get component name)
4. Detect protection patterns (conditional rendering with auth checks)
5. Track nesting depth for layout hierarchy

### Challenges
- Nested routes (routes inside layout components)
- Dynamic route segments (:id, :slug)
- Catch-all routes (/*)
- Conditional rendering for auth (ternary expressions)
- Routes defined across multiple files (MainApp imports sub-routes)
- Index routes (no path, just index)

### What to extract per Page
- URL pattern → node name (/jobs/:id)
- Component name → label (JobDetail)
- Component file path → source_file
- isProtected: boolean (behind auth guard)
- parentRoute: string | null (nesting hierarchy)
- layoutComponent: string | null (wrapping layout)

### Multi-file strategy
Start with single-file parsing (App.tsx). For sub-routes defined in other files, add a warning that manual enrichment may be needed. Future versions can follow component imports.

## Testing

- [ ] Scanner parses simple \`<Route path="/foo" element={<Foo />} />\` → correct Page node
- [ ] Scanner handles dynamic segments (:id) in path
- [ ] Scanner detects protected routes (behind auth conditional)
- [ ] Scanner handles catch-all routes (/*)
- [ ] Scanner tracks nesting hierarchy (parent routes)
- [ ] Scanner extracts component names from JSX element attributes
- [ ] Scanner generates deterministic IDs: \`page:{urlPattern}\`
- [ ] Scanner handles index routes (no path attribute)
- [ ] Scanner handles Route elements with children (nested Routes)
- [ ] Scanner warns about routes in separate files it can't follow
- [ ] Integration test: parse actual hourly App.tsx

## Validation

- [ ] All top-level hourly routes are captured
- [ ] Protected vs public route status is correctly detected
- [ ] Route nesting hierarchy is preserved
- [ ] Scan completes in under 2 seconds
- [ ] Scanner implements the Scanner interface correctly

- packages/core/src/__tests__/scanner/router-scanner.test.ts
- packages/core/src/__tests__/fixtures/App.tsx (test fixture)

- Creates edges: uses_route, touches_entity, on_page

{"description":"## What

Implement the tiered match algorithm in Go — the core feature that makes BDD work. Three tiers: Cucumber expression match → FTS5 fuzzy match → LLM-assisted Action creation suggestion.

## Context

Uses cucumber-expressions-go for Tier 1 (exact matching with parameter extraction), SQLite FTS5 for Tier 2 (BM25 ranked fuzzy search), and structured context packaging for Tier 3 (suggestion builder).

Tier 3 is the GROWTH mechanism: when no Action matches, the algorithm queries the graph for related Routes/Entities/Pages and packages a CreateActionInput suggestion. The LLM (Claude via MCP) reviews and persists it. The graph learns from every new feature.

## Implementation

### MatchService (internal/match/matcher.go)
\`\`\`go
type MatchService struct {
    repo    *graph.GraphRepository
    actions *graph.ActionService
    opts    MatchOptions
}

func (m *MatchService) Match(stepText string, keyword *string) *MatchResult
func (m *MatchService) MatchScenario(steps []Step) []MatchResult
\`\`\`

### MatchResult
\`\`\`go
type MatchResult struct {
    Tier       string          // "exact", "fuzzy", "suggest"
    Action     *ActionNode     // Tier 1
    Parameters map[string]string // Tier 1 extracted params
    Candidates []FuzzyCandidate  // Tier 2
    Suggestion *CreateActionInput // Tier 3
    Context    *RelatedContext   // Tier 3
}
\`\`\`

### CucumberMatcher (internal/match/cucumber.go)
- Uses cucumber-expressions-go to compile patterns and match step text
- Expression caching for performance
- Parameter extraction from matched steps

### FuzzyMatcher (internal/match/fuzzy.go)
- Tokenizes step text (removes Given/When/Then, articles)
- Queries FTS5 with BM25 ranking

### Integration

{"id":"aba-zca.10","title":"Phase 4: CLI Foundation & Init Command","description":"## What

Implement the Commander.js CLI skeleton with the \`abacus init\` command, global options, and the binary entry point. This provides the user-facing interface that all other CLI commands build on.

## Context

The CLI is the primary human interface to Abacus. It uses Commander.js with @commander-js/extra-typings for type-safe argument parsing. The binary is registered as both \`abacus\` and \`ab\` in package.json.

### Global Options (all commands)
- \`--json\` — output in JSON format (for agent consumption)
- \`--db <path>\` — override database path (default: .abacus/abacus.db)
- \`--quiet\` — suppress non-essential output
- \`--verbose\` — enable debug logging

### Init Command
\`abacus init [--project-name <name>]\`
1. Create .abacus/ directory
2. Create abacus.db with schema
3. Auto-detect project type:
   - Look for prisma schema in common locations
   - Look for oRPC contract patterns
   - Look for React Router config
4. Generate .abacus/config.yaml with detected paths
5. Add .abacus/abacus.db to .gitignore (DB is local, config is committed)
6. Print summary of detected scanners

## Implementation

### Entry Point (packages/cli/src/index.ts)
\`\`\`typescript
#!/usr/bin/env node
import { Command } from '@commander-js/extra-typings';
import { initCommand } from './commands/init.js';

const program = new Command()
  .name('abacus')
  .description('Living application graph for Gherkin-driven BDD')
  .version('0.1.0')
  .option('--json', 'Output in JSON format', false)
  .option('--db <path>', 'Database path', '.abacus/abacus.db')
  .option('-q, --quiet', 'Suppress non-essential output', false)
  .option('-v, --verbose', 'Enable debug logging', false);

program.addCommand(initCommand);
// Future: scanCommand, routesCommand, entitiesCommand, etc.

program.parse();
\`\`\`

### Init Command (packages/cli/src/commands/init.ts)
- Auto-detect project structure by searching for common file patterns
- Generate config.yaml with detected scanner paths
- Create DB and run schema initialization
- Output: JSON summary or human-readable table

### Output Formatting
Create a shared output module:
\`\`\`typescript
// packages/cli/src/output.ts
export function output(data: unknown, options: { json: boolean }): void {
  if (options.json) {
    console.log(JSON.stringify(data, null, 2));
  } else {
    // Human-readable format
    console.log(formatHuman(data));
  }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] \`abacus --version\` prints version
- [ ] \`abacus --help\` prints help with all commands
- [ ] \`abacus init\` creates .abacus/ directory
- [ ] \`abacus init\` creates abacus.db with correct schema

- [ ] \`abacus init --project-name foo\` sets project name in config
- [ ] Auto-detection finds Prisma schema in standard locations
- [ ] Auto-detection finds oRPC contracts in standard locations
- [ ] Global --db flag overrides default database path

## Validation

- packages/cli/src/index.ts

- packages/cli/src/output.ts
- packages/cli/src/__tests__/init.test.ts

{"id":"aba-zca.11","title":"Phase 4: CLI Scan & Query Commands","description":"## What

Implement the CLI scan command and all query commands (routes, entities, pages, actions, graph). These are the primary day-to-day commands users and agents interact with.

## Context

These commands consume the scanner registry, graph repository, and action service. They follow the same pattern: parse args → call core service → format output.

### Commands

**\`abacus scan [type]\`**
- \`abacus scan\` — run all enabled scanners
- \`abacus scan routes\` — run only oRPC scanner
- \`abacus scan entities\` — run only Prisma scanner
- \`abacus scan pages\` — run only Router scanner
- Options: \`--dry-run\` (show what would change), \`--force\` (ignore scan hashes)
- Output: SyncReport (added, updated, removed, unchanged, stale actions)

**\`abacus routes [--match <pattern>] [--method <METHOD>] [--tag <tag>]\`**
- List all Route nodes, optionally filtered
- \`--match\` does FTS5 search on route name/path
- \`--method GET\` filters by HTTP method
- \`--tag Auth\` filters by tag
- Output: table or JSON of routes

**\`abacus entities [name] [--match <pattern>] [--fields]\`**
- List all Entity nodes, optionally filtered
- \`abacus entities User\` shows detailed view with fields and relations
- \`--fields\` includes field list in table output
- \`--match\` does FTS5 search

**\`abacus pages [--match <pattern>] [--protected]\`**
- List all Page nodes, optionally filtered
- \`--protected\` shows only auth-protected pages

**\`abacus actions [--match <pattern>] [--route <id>] [--entity <id>]\`**
- List all Action nodes
- \`--match\` searches by name/description
- Filter by associated route or entity

**\`abacus action create <name> --gherkin <pattern> [--route <id>] [--entities <ids>] [--page <id>]\`**
- Create a new Action node interactively or with flags
- Validates references against graph

**\`abacus graph <node-id> [--depth <n>]\`**
- Show everything connected to a node
- Output: formatted subgraph (nodes + edges)
- Default depth: 2

**\`abacus stats\`**
- Summary: node counts by kind, edge counts, action coverage, scan staleness

## Testing

- [ ] \`abacus scan\` runs all enabled scanners and prints sync report
- [ ] \`abacus scan routes\` runs only oRPC scanner
- [ ] \`abacus scan --dry-run\` shows changes without persisting

- [ ] \`abacus entities User\` shows detailed entity view
- [ ] \`abacus entities --fields\` includes field info in list
- [ ] \`abacus pages --protected\` shows only protected pages

- [ ] \`abacus stats\` shows correct counts

NOTE: This bead touches many files in packages/cli/src/commands/. {"id":"aba-zca.12","title":"Phase 4: CLI Match Command","description":"## What

Implement the CLI match command that lets users and agents match Gherkin step text to Actions using the tiered match algorithm. Also supports matching full scenarios from .feature files.

## Context

This is the user-facing interface to the tiered match algorithm (aba-zca.9). It supports:
1. Single step matching: \`abacus match "I submit my timesheet for January"\`
2. File-based matching: \`abacus match --file path/to/feature.feature\`
3. Interactive creation: when Tier 3 returns a suggestion, prompt the user to create the Action

### Commands

**\`abacus match <step-text>\`**
- Match a single Gherkin step text
- Output varies by tier:
  - Tier 1 (exact): Action details + extracted parameters
  - Tier 2 (fuzzy): Ranked candidate list
  - Tier 3 (suggest): Suggested Action with graph context
- Options:
  - \`--keyword <Given|When|Then>\` — Gherkin keyword context
  - \`--create\` — auto-create suggested Action (Tier 3 only)
  - \`--threshold <0-1>\` — fuzzy match threshold (default 0.3)

**\`abacus match --file <feature-file>\`**
- Parse a .feature file with @cucumber/gherkin
- Match each step against the graph
- Output: coverage report showing matched/unmatched steps
- Options:
  - \`--scenario <name>\` — match only a specific scenario
  - \`--create-all\` — auto-create Actions for all unmatched steps

**\`abacus coverage [glob]\`**
- Run match against all .feature files matching glob
- Output: coverage summary (% of steps with exact matches, fuzzy matches, no matches)

## Testing

- [ ] \`abacus match "I log in"\` returns Tier 1 result when Action exists
- [ ] \`abacus match "I log in"\` returns Tier 2 candidates when fuzzy matches exist
- [ ] \`abacus match "I do something entirely new"\` returns Tier 3 suggestion
- [ ] \`abacus match --create\` creates Action from suggestion
- [ ] \`abacus match --file login.feature\` parses and matches all steps
- [ ] \`abacus match --file login.feature --json\` outputs structured results
- [ ] \`abacus coverage "e2e/**/*.feature"\` produces coverage report
- [ ] Coverage report shows correct percentages

## Validation

- [ ] Single step matching works for all three tiers
- [ ] File matching parses real .feature files correctly
- [ ] Coverage report is accurate against known graph state
- [ ] JSON output is complete and parseable

## Files

- packages/cli/src/commands/match.ts
- packages/cli/src/commands/coverage.ts
- packages/cli/src/__tests__/match.test.ts

## Dependencies

{"description":"## What

Implement the MCP server using the official Go MCP SDK (github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk). Exposes the Abacus graph to Claude Code agents via stdio transport.

## Context

The MCP server is built into the same binary as the CLI: \`abacus mcp-server\` starts the MCP server on stdio. Uses the Go SDK's mcp.NewServer, mcp.AddTool, and StdioTransport.

Tool input schemas are auto-generated from Go struct tags + jsonschema annotations.

## Implementation

### Entry Point
\`abacus mcp-server\` cobra command starts the MCP server:
\`\`\`go
func init() { rootCmd.AddCommand(mcpServerCmd) }

var mcpServerCmd = &cobra.Command{
    Use:   "mcp-server",
    Short: "Start MCP server for Claude Code integration",
    RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
        return mcp.StartServer()
    },
}
\`\`\`

### Tool Registration (mcp/server.go)
\`\`\`go
func StartServer() error {
    server := mcp.NewServer(&mcp.Implementation{
        Name: "abacus", Version: "0.1.0",
    }, nil)

    // Register all tools
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_scan", ...}, scanHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_query_routes", ...}, queryRoutesHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_query_entities", ...}, queryEntitiesHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_query_pages", ...}, queryPagesHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_query_actions", ...}, queryActionsHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_create_action", ...}, createActionHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_update_action", ...}, updateActionHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_match_gherkin", ...}, matchGherkinHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_match_scenario", ...}, matchScenarioHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_graph_context", ...}, graphContextHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_stats", ...}, statsHandler)

    return server.Run(context.Background(), &mcp.StdioTransport{})
}
\`\`\`

### Claude Code Configuration
\`\`\`json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "abacus": {
      "command": "abacus",
      "args": ["mcp-server"],
      "cwd": "/path/to/target/project"
    }
  }
}
\`\`\`

### Tool Input Types
Each tool handler has a Go struct with jsonschema tags:
\`\`\`go
type MatchGherkinInput struct {
    StepText   string  \`json:"stepText" jsonschema:"the Gherkin step text to match"\`
    Keyword    *string \`json:"keyword,omitempty" jsonschema:"Given, When, Then, or And"\`
    AutoCreate bool    \`json:"autoCreate,omitempty" jsonschema:"auto-create Action if no match found"\`
}
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] Server starts on stdio
- [ ] All 11 tools registered and callable
- [ ] Tool input validation via struct tags
- [ ] Query tools return correct filtered results
- [ ] Match tool returns correct tier results
- [ ] Create action tool validates and persists
- [ ] No stdout contamination (all logs to stderr)
- [ ] Server startup < 500ms

## Validation

- [ ] Configurable in Claude Code settings
- [ ] All tools callable from Claude Code
- [ ] Responses properly formatted
- [ ] Single binary — no separate MCP server install

## Files

- mcp/server.go
- mcp/tools.go (tool handlers)
- mcp/resources.go
- mcp/server_test.go
- internal/cli/mcp_server.go (cobra command)

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.7, aba-zca.8, aba-zca.9, aba-zca.3"}

{"description":"## What

Implement the Express Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-express) that parses Express route files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON. This scanner captures the **primary backend route infrastructure** — Express is the actual server, not a legacy layer.

## Context

The hourly backend is an Express application. oRPC is used for contract definition and client SDK generation, but Express is the runtime route infrastructure. This means:

- **Express scanner** captures: actual route registration, middleware chains, auth guards, rate limiters, route mounting hierarchy, and routes that may not have oRPC contracts
- **oRPC scanner** captures: typed API contracts (input/output schemas, tags, summaries) used for client generation

Both scanners are essential and complementary. Together they give a complete picture:
- oRPC tells you WHAT the API contract looks like (types, schemas)
- Express tells you HOW the route is actually wired (middleware, guards, mounting)

The backend has 30+ route files in backend/src/routes/.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/express/
- Use ts-morph to find Express route patterns
- Extract: HTTP method, path, middleware chain, handler function name
- Handle route prefixes from app.use() mounting
- Node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{fullPath}\`

### Express Route Patterns to Detect
\`\`\`typescript
// Direct routes
router.get('/users', getUsers);
router.post('/users', validateBody, createUser);

// Parameterized routes
router.get('/users/:id', getUserById);

// Route mounting (prefix composition)
app.use('/api/v1', userRouter);  // all routes get /api/v1 prefix

// Route chaining
router.route('/users/:id')
  .get(getUser)
  .put(updateUser)
  .delete(deleteUser);

// Middleware stacks (capture these — they reveal auth, validation, rate limiting)
router.post('/admin/users', requireAuth, requireRole('admin'), rateLimiter, createUser);
\`\`\`

### Middleware Extraction
Middleware names are valuable context for Actions — they reveal:
- Auth requirements (requireAuth, requireRole)
- Validation (validateBody, validateParams)
- Rate limiting (rateLimiter)
- File handling (upload.single)

Store as array in properties: \`"middleware": ["requireAuth", "requireRole", "validateBody"]\`

### Relationship to oRPC Scanner
When both scanners run, Abacus will have two Route nodes for the same endpoint — one from oRPC (contract) and one from Express (runtime). The CLI scan command should detect these overlaps and create \`relates_to\` edges between them. This is handled by the SyncScanResults logic in the Go core, not by this scanner.

## Testing

- [ ] Parses router.get/post/put/delete → correct Route nodes
- [ ] Handles parameterized routes (:id, :slug)
- [ ] Extracts middleware chain names
- [ ] Detects app.use() prefix mounting and composes full paths
- [ ] Handles router.route() chaining
- [ ] Output conforms to Scanner Protocol v1
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly Express route files

## Validation

- [ ] All 30+ hourly Express route files scanned
- [ ] Middleware chains captured (reveals auth/validation requirements)
- [ ] Route mounting prefixes correctly composed
- [ ] Valid Scanner Protocol v1 output

## Files

- scanners/express/package.json, tsconfig.json
- scanners/express/src/index.ts, src/parser.ts
- scanners/express/__tests__/

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.3 (Scanner Protocol v1 schema)"}

Implement the Express Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-express) that parses Express route files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON.

## Context

BYOASTP scanner for legacy Express routes. The target codebase has 30+ Express route files in backend/src/routes/ that are being migrated to oRPC. This scanner captures the routes that haven't been migrated yet.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/express/
- Use ts-morph to find Express route patterns:
  - \`router.get('/path', handler)\`
  - \`router.post('/path', middleware, handler)\`
  - \`app.use('/prefix', router)\`
- Extract: HTTP method, path, middleware chain, handler function name
- Handle route prefixes from app.use() mounting
- Node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{fullPath}\`

### Express Route Patterns to Detect
\`\`\`typescript
// Direct routes
router.get('/users', getUsers);
router.post('/users', validateBody, createUser);

// Parameterized routes
router.get('/users/:id', getUserById);

// Route mounting (prefix)
app.use('/api/v1', userRouter);  // all routes in userRouter get /api/v1 prefix

// Route chaining
router.route('/users/:id')
  .get(getUser)
  .put(updateUser)
  .delete(deleteUser);
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] Parses router.get/post/put/delete → correct Route nodes
- [ ] Handles parameterized routes (:id)
- [ ] Detects middleware in route chain
- [ ] Handles app.use() prefix mounting
- [ ] Handles router.route() chaining
- [ ] Output conforms to Scanner Protocol v1
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly Express routes

## Validation

- [ ] Legacy Express routes captured alongside oRPC routes
- [ ] No duplicate IDs with oRPC scanner output
- [ ] Valid Scanner Protocol v1 output

## Files

- scanners/express/package.json, tsconfig.json
- scanners/express/src/index.ts, src/parser.ts
- scanners/express/__tests__/

## Dependencies

{"id":"aba-zca","title":"Abacus: Living Application Graph + Gherkin-driven BDD","description":"Build Abacus: a standalone SQLite-backed graph tool that models an application's API surface, entities, pages, and user actions. Serves as the persistent, cumulative codebase understanding layer that bridges Gherkin specs to implementation.

Linear project: Abacus (pcom-mike workspace)
Linear issue: https://linear.app/pcom-mike/issue/ABA-1/brainstorm-abacus-living-application-graph-gherkin-driven-bdd

## Architecture

- **Language**: Go (single binary, excellent compiler feedback, matches beads tooling)
- **Database**: SQLite (.abacus/abacus.db) per target project via modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go, no CGo)
- **CLI**: cobra (same as beads)
- **MCP Server**: github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk (official Go SDK)
- **Graph Model**: Adjacency list (nodes + edges tables) with JSONB properties, FTS5 search
- **Node Types**: Route, Entity, Page, Action, Permission

## BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) Scanner Architecture

Abacus does NOT parse source code itself. Instead:
- Abacus defines a **Scanner Protocol** — a JSON schema for scan results
- Scanners are **standalone executables** that output JSON conforming to the protocol
- Abacus spawns scanners as subprocesses, reads their JSON output, ingests into SQLite
- Config (.abacus/config.yaml) maps scanner IDs to executable commands

This means:
- Abacus core is 100% Go — no TypeScript, no AST parsing, no language-specific deps
- Scanners can be written in ANY language (Node.js for TS, Python for Django, etc.)
- We ship 4 reference scanners as npm packages (oRPC, Prisma, React Router, Express)
- Community can write scanners for any stack

## Project Structure
\`\`\`
abacus/
├── go.mod / go.sum
├── cmd/abacus/main.go           # CLI + MCP server entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── db/                      # SQLite connection, schema, migrations
│   ├── graph/                   # GraphRepository, ActionService
│   ├── match/                   # MatchService, CucumberMatcher, FuzzyMatcher, SuggestionBuilder
│   ├── scanner/                 # Scanner protocol types, subprocess runner
│   └── cli/                     # Cobra command definitions
├── mcp/                         # MCP server tool/resource registration
├── scanners/                    # Reference scanner implementations (Node.js)
│   ├── orpc/                    # @abacus/scanner-orpc
│   ├── prisma/                  # @abacus/scanner-prisma
│   ├── react-router/            # @abacus/scanner-react-router
│   └── express/                 # @abacus/scanner-express
└── testdata/                    # Test fixtures
\`\`\`

## Technology Stack
- Go 1.23+ (single binary distribution)
- modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go SQLite, no CGo)
- github.com/spf13/cobra (CLI framework)
- github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk (MCP server)
- github.com/cucumber/cucumber-expressions-go (pattern matching)
- github.com/cucumber/gherkin/go (Gherkin parsing)
- Node.js scanner packages: ts-morph, @prisma/internals, @cucumber/gherkin

## Key Decisions
1. Go for core — single binary, great compiler messages, matches beads ecosystem
2. BYOASTP — scanners are standalone executables, not embedded parsers
3. .abacus/ directory lives in the TARGET project (like .beads/)
4. Scanner Protocol is the interface — JSON in/out, language-agnostic
5. Match algorithm is a GROWTH mechanism — LLM fallback creates new Actions
6. 4 reference scanners ship as npm packages for the hourly stack
7. Official Go MCP SDK for Claude Code integration

## Phases
1. Foundation (scaffolding, schema, scanner protocol)
2. Scanners (oRPC, Prisma, Router, Express — Node.js, parallelizable)
3. Core Features (query layer, actions, match algorithm)
4. Interfaces (CLI commands, MCP server)
5. Integration (E2E validation against hourly codebase)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"mike@seedless.club","created_at":"2026-03-06T20:49:55Z","created_by":"mjn","updated_at":"2026-03-06T21:13:29Z"}

{"id":"aba-zca.1","title":"Phase 1: Project Scaffolding & Configuration","description":"## What

Set up the Abacus Go module with directory structure, cobra CLI skeleton, build tooling, and configuration schema. This is the foundation all other beads build on.

## Context

Abacus is a Go project producing a single binary. It uses cobra for CLI (same as beads), modernc.org/sqlite for the database, and the official Go MCP SDK. The .abacus/ directory lives in the TARGET project, not in the Abacus repo.

## Implementation

### Directory Structure
\`\`\`
abacus/
├── go.mod                          # module github.com/mjn/abacus
├── go.sum
├── Makefile                        # build, test, install targets
├── cmd/abacus/main.go              # entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── db/                         # SQLite layer
│   ├── graph/                      # Graph operations
│   ├── match/                      # Match algorithm
│   ├── scanner/                    # Scanner protocol + runner
│   └── cli/                        # Cobra commands
├── mcp/                            # MCP server
├── scanners/                       # Reference Node.js scanners
│   ├── orpc/
│   ├── prisma/
│   ├── react-router/
│   └── express/
└── testdata/                       # Test fixtures
\`\`\`

### go.mod
\`\`\`go
module github.com/mjn/abacus

go 1.23

require (
    github.com/spf13/cobra v1.9.1
    modernc.org/sqlite v1.37.1
    github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk v1.4.0
    github.com/cucumber/cucumber-expressions-go v0.0.0
    github.com/cucumber/gherkin/go/v38 v38.0.0
)
\`\`\`

### Makefile
- \`make build\` — \`go build -o bin/abacus ./cmd/abacus\`
- \`make install\` — \`go install ./cmd/abacus\`
- \`make test\` — \`go test ./...\`
- \`make lint\` — \`golangci-lint run\`

### Entry Point (cmd/abacus/main.go)
\`\`\`go
package main

import (
    "os"
    "github.com/mjn/abacus/internal/cli"
)

func main() {
    if err := cli.Execute(); err != nil {
        os.Exit(1)
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Configuration Schema (.abacus/config.yaml)
\`\`\`yaml
version: 1
project:
  name: "hourly"
  root: "."
scanners:
  orpc:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-orpc"
    options:
      contractPaths: ["shared/api-client/src/contracts/**/*.ts"]
      handlerPaths: ["backend/src/orpc/**/*.handlers.ts"]
  prisma:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-prisma"
    options:
      schemaPath: "backend/prisma/schema.prisma"
  react-router:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-react-router"
    options:
      configPath: "frontend/src/App.tsx"
      pagesDir: "frontend/src/pages"
  express:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-express"
    options:
      routePaths: ["backend/src/routes/**/*.ts"]
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] \`go build ./...\` compiles without errors
- [ ] \`go vet ./...\` passes
- [ ] \`make build\` produces bin/abacus binary
- [ ] \`make install\` installs abacus to GOPATH/bin
- [ ] \`abacus --version\` prints version
- [ ] \`abacus --help\` prints help
- [ ] Config YAML parsing works for valid config
- [ ] Config parsing rejects invalid YAML with clear errors

## Validation

- [ ] Single binary builds in < 5 seconds
- [ ] Binary size is reasonable (< 30MB)
- [ ] All directories exist and Go packages compile
- [ ] No CGo dependencies (pure Go)

## Files

- go.mod, go.sum
- Makefile
- cmd/abacus/main.go
- internal/cli/root.go
- internal/config/config.go (config parsing)
- .gitignore

## Dependencies

None — this is the first bead.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mike@seedless.club","created_at":"2026-03-06T20:50:19Z","created_by":"mjn","updated_at":"2026-03-06T21:13:51Z"}

{"id":"aba-zca.2","title":"Phase 1: Core Types & SQLite Schema","description":"## What

Design and implement the SQLite database schema and Go type system for the Abacus application graph. Includes all table definitions, indexes, FTS5, and the DB connection module.

## Context

Same SQL schema as before — the database design is language-independent. The Go layer uses modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go, standard database/sql interface). WAL mode for concurrent reads, NORMAL synchronous for CLI speed.

## Implementation

### Go Types (internal/db/types.go)
\`\`\`go
package db

type NodeKind string
const (
    NodeRoute      NodeKind = "route"
    NodeEntity     NodeKind = "entity"
    NodePage       NodeKind = "page"
    NodeAction     NodeKind = "action"
    NodePermission NodeKind = "permission"
)

type NodeSource string
const (
    SourceScan   NodeSource = "scan"
    SourceAgent  NodeSource = "agent"
    SourceManual NodeSource = "manual"
)

type EdgeKind string
const (
    EdgeUsesRoute          EdgeKind = "uses_route"
    EdgeTouchesEntity      EdgeKind = "touches_entity"
    EdgeOnPage             EdgeKind = "on_page"
    EdgeRequiresPermission EdgeKind = "requires_permission"
    EdgeRelatesTo          EdgeKind = "relates_to"
    EdgeFieldRelation      EdgeKind = "field_relation"
)

type GraphNode struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    Kind       NodeKind       \`json:"kind"\`
    Name       string         \`json:"name"\`
    Label      string         \`json:"label"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    Source     NodeSource     \`json:"source"\`
    SourceFile *string        \`json:"source_file"\`
    CreatedAt  int64          \`json:"created_at"\`
    UpdatedAt  int64          \`json:"updated_at"\`
    ScanHash   *string        \`json:"scan_hash"\`
}

type GraphEdge struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    SrcID      string         \`json:"src_id"\`
    DstID      string         \`json:"dst_id"\`
    Kind       EdgeKind       \`json:"kind"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    CreatedAt  int64          \`json:"created_at"\`
}
\`\`\`

### DB Connection (internal/db/db.go)
- OpenDB(path string) (*sql.DB, error) — open with WAL mode, foreign keys, cache
- InitSchema(db *sql.DB) error — create tables, indexes, FTS5, triggers
- CloseDB(db *sql.DB) error — graceful close
- Properties stored as JSON TEXT (modernc.org/sqlite supports json_extract)

### SQL Schema
Same as original plan — nodes, edges, nodes_fts, triggers, schema_version. See epic description for full DDL.

## Testing

- [ ] OpenDB creates database with correct pragmas
- [ ] InitSchema creates all tables and indexes
- [ ] Node CRUD operations work for all 5 kinds
- [ ] Edge CRUD with UNIQUE constraint enforcement
- [ ] FTS5 search with BM25 ranking
- [ ] JSON properties round-trip correctly
- [ ] Cascading edge deletion on node delete
- [ ] Schema versioning works
- [ ] Transaction rollback on error

## Validation

- [ ] All 5 node types stored and retrieved correctly
- [ ] Edges maintain referential integrity
- [ ] FTS5 returns relevant results
- [ ] Pure Go — no CGo compilation required

## Files

- internal/db/types.go
- internal/db/db.go
- internal/db/schema.go (DDL strings)
- internal/db/db_test.go

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.1 (Go module must exist)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mike@seedless.club","created_at":"2026-03-06T20:51:03Z","created_by":"mjn","updated_at":"2026-03-06T21:14:11Z"}

{"id":"aba-zca.3","title":"Phase 1: Pluggable Scanner Architecture","description":"## What

Design and implement the Scanner Protocol — the BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) interface that defines how external scanner executables communicate with Abacus. This is the most architecturally important bead: it defines the contract that makes scanners language-agnostic.

## Context

Abacus does NOT parse source code. Instead, scanners are standalone executables that:
1. Receive configuration via stdin (JSON)
2. Parse source files in whatever language they understand
3. Output a ScanResult JSON document to stdout
4. Exit 0 on success, non-zero on failure (errors to stderr)

The Scanner Protocol is a versioned JSON schema. Abacus ships v1; future versions maintain backward compatibility.

## Implementation

### Scanner Protocol v1 — The Interface

**Input (stdin → scanner):**
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": 1,
  "projectRoot": "/absolute/path/to/project",
  "options": {
    "contractPaths": ["shared/api-client/src/contracts/**/*.ts"]
  }
}
\`\`\`

**Output (scanner → stdout):**
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": 1,
  "scanner": {
    "id": "orpc",
    "name": "oRPC Route Scanner",
    "version": "0.1.0"
  },
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "route:POST-/api/auth/register",
      "kind": "route",
      "name": "POST /api/auth/register",
      "label": "Register a new user",
      "properties": {
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/api/auth/register",
        "summary": "Register a new user",
        "tags": ["Auth"],
        "inputSchema": "RegisterInputSchema",
        "outputSchema": "AuthResponseSchema",
        "successStatus": 201,
        "contractFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts",
        "handlerFile": "backend/src/orpc/auth.handlers.ts"
      },
      "source": "scan",
      "sourceFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts"
    }
  ],
  "edges": [
    {
      "id": "edge:entity:User-field_relation-entity:Company",
      "srcId": "entity:User",
      "dstId": "entity:Company",
      "kind": "field_relation",
      "properties": {
        "fieldName": "company",
        "relationType": "many-to-one"
      }
    }
  ],
  "warnings": [
    {
      "file": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/broken.ts",
      "line": 42,
      "message": "Failed to parse: unexpected token",
      "severity": "warn"
    }
  ],
  "stats": {
    "filesScanned": 45,
    "nodesFound": 47,
    "edgesFound": 12,
    "errors": 1,
    "durationMs": 1234
  }
}
\`\`\`

### Go Types for Protocol (internal/scanner/protocol.go)

\`\`\`go
package scanner

// ScanInput is sent to scanner executables via stdin
type ScanInput struct {
    Version     int            \`json:"version"\`
    ProjectRoot string         \`json:"projectRoot"\`
    Options     map[string]any \`json:"options"\`
}

// ScanOutput is received from scanner executables via stdout
type ScanOutput struct {
    Version  int            \`json:"version"\`
    Scanner  ScannerInfo    \`json:"scanner"\`
    Nodes    []ScanNode     \`json:"nodes"\`
    Edges    []ScanEdge     \`json:"edges"\`
    Warnings []ScanWarning  \`json:"warnings"\`
    Stats    ScanStats      \`json:"stats"\`
}

type ScannerInfo struct {
    ID      string \`json:"id"\`
    Name    string \`json:"name"\`
    Version string \`json:"version"\`
}

type ScanNode struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    Kind       string         \`json:"kind"\`   // must be valid NodeKind
    Name       string         \`json:"name"\`
    Label      string         \`json:"label"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    Source     string         \`json:"source"\` // always "scan" for scanners
    SourceFile string         \`json:"sourceFile,omitempty"\`
}

type ScanEdge struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    SrcID      string         \`json:"srcId"\`
    DstID      string         \`json:"dstId"\`
    Kind       string         \`json:"kind"\`   // must be valid EdgeKind
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties,omitempty"\`
}

type ScanWarning struct {
    File     string \`json:"file"\`
    Line     *int   \`json:"line,omitempty"\`
    Message  string \`json:"message"\`
    Severity string \`json:"severity"\` // "info", "warn", "error"
}

type ScanStats struct {
    FilesScanned int   \`json:"filesScanned"\`
    NodesFound   int   \`json:"nodesFound"\`
    EdgesFound   int   \`json:"edgesFound"\`
    Errors       int   \`json:"errors"\`
    DurationMs   int64 \`json:"durationMs"\`
}
\`\`\`

### Scanner Runner (internal/scanner/runner.go)

\`\`\`go
package scanner

// Runner spawns scanner executables and collects results
type Runner struct {
    timeout time.Duration  // default: 60s
}

// RunScanner spawns a scanner executable, sends input via stdin, reads output from stdout
func (r *Runner) RunScanner(ctx context.Context, command string, input ScanInput) (*ScanOutput, error)

// RunAll runs all configured scanners (sequentially or parallel) and merges results
func (r *Runner) RunAll(ctx context.Context, configs []ScannerConfig) (*MergedScanOutput, error)

// ValidateOutput checks that scanner output conforms to protocol v1
func ValidateOutput(output *ScanOutput) []string  // returns validation errors
\`\`\`

**RunScanner implementation:**
1. Parse command string into executable + args
2. Create exec.CommandContext with timeout
3. Pipe ScanInput JSON to stdin
4. Capture stdout (ScanOutput JSON) and stderr (error messages)
5. Parse and validate the JSON output
6. Return ScanOutput or error with stderr contents

**Error handling:**
- Scanner exits non-zero → return error with stderr
- Scanner produces invalid JSON → return parse error with raw output
- Scanner times out → kill process, return timeout error
- Scanner outputs valid JSON with warnings → return output + log warnings

### Protocol Validation Rules
- version must be 1
- scanner.id must be non-empty
- All nodes must have id, kind, name, label
- kind must be one of: route, entity, page, action, permission
- All edges must reference node IDs that exist in the nodes array
- Edge kind must be valid EdgeKind
- No duplicate node IDs

### JSON Schema File
Generate a JSON Schema file (protocol/scanner-v1.schema.json) that scanner authors can validate against. This serves as documentation AND validation tool.

## Testing

- [ ] RunScanner spawns a test executable and reads its output
- [ ] RunScanner sends ScanInput JSON via stdin correctly
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner exit code 0 → success
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner exit code 1 → error with stderr
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner timeout → kills process
- [ ] RunScanner handles invalid JSON output → clear error message
- [ ] ValidateOutput accepts valid ScanOutput
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects output with missing required fields
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects output with invalid node kinds
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects edges referencing nonexistent nodes
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects duplicate node IDs
- [ ] RunAll runs multiple scanners and merges results
- [ ] RunAll continues when one scanner fails, reports error in warnings
- [ ] ScanInput JSON serialization is correct
- [ ] ScanOutput JSON deserialization handles all field types
- [ ] Test with a simple bash scanner: \`echo '{"version":1,...}'\`

## Validation

- [ ] Protocol is simple enough that a bash one-liner can be a valid scanner
- [ ] Protocol JSON schema is valid and documents all fields
- [ ] Runner handles real-world edge cases (large output, slow scanners)
- [ ] No Go-specific assumptions in the protocol (truly language-agnostic)
- [ ] Merged output from multiple scanners has no duplicate nodes

## Files

- internal/scanner/protocol.go (types)
- internal/scanner/runner.go (subprocess execution)
- internal/scanner/validate.go (output validation)
- internal/scanner/runner_test.go
- internal/scanner/validate_test.go
- protocol/scanner-v1.schema.json (JSON Schema for scanner authors)
- testdata/scanners/echo-scanner.sh (test fixture)

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.2 (needs db types for NodeKind, EdgeKind validation)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mike@seedless.club","created_at":"2026-03-06T20:51:40Z","created_by":"mjn","updated_at":"2026-03-06T21:14:54Z"}

{"id":"aba-zca.10","title":"Phase 4: CLI Foundation & Init Command","description":"## What

Implement the cobra CLI skeleton with the \`abacus init\` command, global flags, and output formatting. Uses cobra (same as beads CLI).

## Context

Single binary entry point. Global flags: --json, --db, --quiet, --verbose. The init command creates .abacus/ directory, generates config.yaml by auto-detecting project structure, and initializes the SQLite database.

## Implementation

\`\`\`go
var rootCmd = &cobra.Command{
    Use:   "abacus",
    Short: "Living application graph for Gherkin-driven BDD",
    Aliases: []string{"ab"},
}

// Global flags
var jsonOutput bool
var dbPath string
var quiet bool
var verbose bool

### Init Command (internal/cli/init.go)
